sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=21).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 20, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ACTION7 and directional keys only advance the progress bar. Clicking green objects produces temporary marks. Need to try clicking on gray cells or other board elements.
- Goal model: Perhaps need to click gray cells to change them, or click in a different pattern.
- Plan: Try clicking on a gray cell to see if it changes. Also try clicking on the border.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 19 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see something interesting! Clicking on the gray cell at (19, 21) between green 1 and green 2 produced:
1. A G border around green 1 (the left green object)
2. g marks to the right, between green 2 and green 3
So clicking between two green objects selects the left one and shows marks toward the right. This is different from clicking directly on a green object.
Looking at the changes:
- Row 17: WWWWWWWWWWWWWWW -> WWGGGGWWWWWWWWW (G border top)
- Row 18: WWwNNwWWwNNwWWw -> WGGNNGGWwNNwWWw (G border sides, green 1 selected)
- Row 19: WWNNNNWWNNNNWWw -> WGNNNNGWNNNNWWg (G border sides, g mark on right)
- Row 20: WWNNNNWWNNNNWWw -> WGNNNNGWNNNNWWg (G border sides, g mark on right)
- Row 21: WWwNNwWWwNNwWWw -> WGGNNGGWwNNwWWw (G border sides)
- Row 22: WWWWWWWWWWWWWWW -> WWGGGGWWWWWWWWW (G border bottom)
So clicking at (19, 21) - which is between green 1 and green 2 - selected green 1 with a G border and placed g marks to the right.
This is very similar to what happened when I clicked directly on green 1! Let me compare:
- Clicking green 1 at (19, 18): G border around green 1, g marks to the right
- Clicking gray cell at (19, 21): G border around green 1, g marks to the right
So clicking between two green objects selects the left one. This suggests the game might be about clicking the gaps between green objects to connect them.
Wait, but earlier when I clicked green 1 at (19, 18), the marks were at positions around (18-21, 29-32), which is near green 3. And now clicking at (19, 21), the marks are at different positions.
Let me look at the current state more carefully. The g marks are at:
- Row 19, col 33: g
- Row 20, col 33: g
That's to the right of green 2 (which is at col 24). So the marks are between green 2 and green 3.
Hmm, so clicking between green 1 and green 2 selected green 1 and placed marks between green 2 and green 3. This seems like the marks are showing where to click next!
So the pattern might be:
1. Click between green 1 and green 2 → selects green 1, marks appear between green 2 and green 3
2. Next, click between green 2 and green 3 → selects green 2, marks appear somewhere else
3. Continue until all greens are selected
Let me try clicking between green 2 and green 3. Green 2 is at (19, 24) and green 3 is at (19, 36). The gap is around col 30-35. Let me try clicking at (19, 30).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the gray cell between green 1 and green 2 at (19, 21) selected green 1 with a G border and placed g marks between green 2 and green 3. The marks seem to indicate where to click next!
Goal model: Click between adjacent green objects to select them in sequence, following the g mark hints.
Plan: Click between green 2 and green 3 (around col 30) as indicated by the g marks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The g marks are at (19, 33) and (20, 33), between green 2 (col 24) and green 3 (col 36)
# Let me click there
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 30}])

# Check what changed
before_ascii = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after_ascii = last_transition.after_frame.ascii.split('\n')
diff_rows = [i for i in range(len(before_ascii)) if before_ascii[i] != after_ascii[i]]
print(f"Changed rows: {diff_rows}")
for r in diff_rows:
    if r != 0:
        print(f"Row {r}: {before_ascii[r][15:40]} -> {after_ascii[r][15:40]}")

# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
g_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] in ('G', 'g')]
print(f"\nG/g nodes: {len(g_nodes)}")
for n in g_nodes:
    print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Row 18: WGGNNGGWwNNwWWwggwWWwNNwW -> WWwwwwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwNNwW
Row 19: WGNNNNGWNNNNWWgwwgWWNNNNW -> WWwwwwWWwwwwWWNNNNWWNNNNW
Row 20: WGNNNNGWNNNNWWgwwgWWNNNNW -> WWwwwwWWwwwwWWNNNNWWNNNNW
Row 21: WGGNNGGWwNNwWWwggwWWwNNwW -> WWwwwwWWwwwwWWwNNwWWwNNwW
